subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Popular Vote Share in Presidential Elections by Party
stimulus: Source: Dave Leip's Atlas of U.S. Presidential Elections, 2016. Bar graph showing Percent of Popular Vote Share in Presidential Elections by Party for Democratic, Republican, and Third-Party/Independent candidates across presidential elections.

parts.prompt: Identify the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent of the popular vote in a presidential election.

scoring_points.description: Identify the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent of the popular vote in a presidential election.

required_elements: The year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent was 1972.
alternatives.correct_example: The year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent was 1972.

parts.prompt: Describe the overall trend in the percent of the popular vote of third-party/independent candidates.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the overall pattern for third-party candidates. The response must describe a trend or pattern, not just a single data point.

scoring_points.description: Describe the overall trend in the percent of the popular vote of third-party/independent candidates.

required_elements: Third-party candidates do not get as high a percentage of the popular vote as major party candidates.
alternatives.correct_example: Third-party candidates do not get as high a percentage of the popular vote as major party candidates.

required_elements: Third-party candidates never win 20% of the popular vote.
alternatives.correct_example: Third-party candidates never win 20% of the popular vote.

required_elements: Third-party candidates never win a majority of the popular vote.
alternatives.correct_example: Third-party candidates never win a majority of the popular vote.

parts.prompt: Using the data, draw a conclusion about how the third-party/independent candidate may have affected the outcome of the 1992 presidential election.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion about impact — not just note that Perot received votes. Accept any plausible inference about how the third-party vote could have affected the electoral outcome.

scoring_points.description: Using the data, draw a conclusion about how the third-party/independent candidate may have affected the outcome of the 1992 presidential election.

required_elements: In 1992, the third-party candidate received a significant number of votes, and if those votes were allocated differently, they could have affected the outcome of the election by changing the number of electors won in the electoral college.
alternatives.correct_example: In 1992, the third-party candidate received a significant number of votes, and if those votes were allocated differently, they could have affected the outcome of the election by changing the number of electors won in the electoral college.

parts.prompt: Explain why the data in the bar graph does not necessarily relate to the outcome of presidential elections.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the electoral college as the mechanism that separates popular vote share from election outcomes.

scoring_points.description: Explain why the data in the bar graph does not necessarily relate to the outcome of presidential elections.

required_elements: The data in the graph do not relate to the outcome because the electoral college, not the popular vote, determines the winner.
alternatives.correct_example: The data in the graph do not relate to the outcome because the electoral college, not the popular vote, determines the winner.

required_elements: The electoral college allows states to choose how they allocate their votes for determining state electors. Most states use a winner-take-all system which sometimes leads to the candidate with the most popular votes not winning the election. Total for question 2
alternatives.correct_example: The electoral college allows states to choose how they allocate their votes for determining state electors. Most states use a winner-take-all system which sometimes leads to the candidate with the most popular votes not winning the election. Total for question 2
